episode_title: Feeling stuck? Here's how to know when it's time to leave your job | Ada Chen Rekhi
insight: Leverage customer advisory councils to gather 'the literal voice of the customer' for product conflicts, enhancing decision-making and influence.
pull_quote: When facing product conflicts, ping top customers directly through a council. Their 'literal voice of the customer' provides rich data, helping resolve debates and strengthening product's position.

episode_title: How to build a high-performing growth team | Adam Fishman (Patreon, Lyft, Imperfect Foods)
insight: Optimized onboarding can shift cohort retention curves outward by 10-20 percentage points by addressing early churn.
pull_quote: Focus on onboarding to significantly improve retention. Companies have shifted cohort retention curves by 10-20 percentage points by optimizing early user experience.

episode_title: When to invest in new acquisition channels | Adam Grenier (Uber, MasterClass)
insight: In changing markets, always re-evaluate product-market fit; your past fit may no longer apply to your current customer base.
pull_quote: Start by assuming you no longer have product market fit, because you had product market fit in a different market. It’s a different market now, so you have to start over.

episode_title: Humanizing product development | Adriel Frederick (Reddit, Lyft, Facebook)
insight: Sustainable growth requires focusing on core user problems and delivering fundamental product value, beyond just short-term hacks.
pull_quote: Growth hacking is valuable for initial traction, but if your product lacks fundamental value, you'll be a one-hit wonder. True growth means relentlessly solving core user problems and grinding on them.

episode_title: Why most AI products fail: Lessons from 50+ AI deployments at OpenAI, Google & Amazon
insight: Begin AI product development with high human control and low agent agency, gradually increasing autonomy as reliability builds.
pull_quote: Don't build fully autonomous AI agents on day one. Start with high human control and low agency, then increase autonomy step-by-step as you gain confidence and build trust with your system.

episode_title: Finding hidden growth opportunities in your product | Albert Cheng (Duolingo, Grammarly, Chess.com)
insight: Sample premium features to free users to demonstrate the full product value, significantly boosting paid subscription conversions.
pull_quote: Grammarly's upgrade rates nearly doubled by interspersing sampled paid suggestions to free users. They then saw Grammarly as a much more powerful tool, converting at higher rates.

episode_title: An inside look at how the New York Times builds product | Alex Hardiman (CPO, the New York Times)
insight: Product teams must balance building bespoke, high-impact experiences for unique content with developing scalable platforms for widespread use.
pull_quote: Many compelling features start as one-off newsroom experiments. Product teams then scale these into repeatable formats, operating in two modes: rapid, in-the-moment storytelling and long-term system building.

episode_title: Thinking like a gardener, slime mold, the adjacent possible: Product advice from Alex Komoroske
insight: Dedicate 70% of effort to clear value, then use the remaining 30% to plant small, experimental "seeds" for future growth.
pull_quote: Dedicate 70% of team effort to obviously useful work to build credibility and minimize questions. Use the remaining 30% to plant 'seeds' – small, experimental projects – that might grow into massive, transformative value.

episode_title: How to drive word of mouth | Nilan Peiris (CPO of Wise)
insight: AI coding agents enable extreme development speed, allowing small teams to launch top-tier products rapidly.
pull_quote: The Sora Android app, a fully new app, was built in 18 days for employees, and then 10 days later for the public, becoming the #1 app in the app store, with just two or three engineers. This is an insane velocity.

episode_title: Scripts for navigating difficult conversations | Alisa Cohn (executive coach)
insight: A leader's job is to drive results and foster growth, not to make employees happy by avoiding essential tough conversations.
pull_quote: Many leaders mistakenly believe their job is to make employees happy, avoiding difficult feedback and hindering growth. This ultimately leads to subpar performance. Your real job is to drive results and enable success.

episode_title: Making an impact through authenticity and curiosity | Ami Vora (CPO at Faire, ex-WhatsApp, FB, IG)
insight: Execution beats strategy; prioritize continually shipping and learning from customers over perfecting plans to truly win in the market.
pull_quote: "Perfect strategy but poor execution, you don't win because your strategy never makes it to the market." Spend 80% of your time on execution, building the machine to constantly improve with customer feedback, not just planning.

episode_title: Behind the product: Replit | Amjad Masad (co-founder and CEO)
insight: As AI makes building faster, a PM's core bottleneck shifts to generating new, valuable ideas and concepts quickly.
pull_quote: Making things is now much easier with AI. The new bottleneck for product managers becomes how fast you can generate valuable ideas and explore new concepts.

episode_title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson
insight: Find profitable niches with low competition to secure early wins and build business muscle effectively.
pull_quote: Fish where the fish are. The biggest mistakes I've made have been going into business models where other people have repeatedly failed and thinking, I can do this better. Competition equals lower margin.

episode_title: When enough is enough | Andy Johns (ex-FB, Twitter, Quora)
insight: Disrupted sleep, strained relationships, or failing physical health are flashing red alarms indicating deeper issues beyond normal work stress.
pull_quote: Normal stress is one thing, but disrupted sleep, strained relationships, or failing physical health are flashing red alarms. Your body signals deeper issues. Don't dismiss these as mere day-to-day work stress; something needs to change.

episode_title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
insight: A strategic narrative defines a movement, providing a clear "north star" to prioritize product features and shape your roadmap.
pull_quote: Traditional pitches focus on problems and solutions, but a strategic narrative defines a new movement, providing a clear north star for product strategy. It helps filter feature requests and prioritizes what to build.

episode_title: The power of strategic narrative | Andy Raskin
insight: Use a clear strategic narrative to filter feature requests and prioritize your product roadmap, aligning all efforts.
pull_quote: Gong's CEO used their strategic narrative to say no to features based on 'opinions.' The narrative acts as a crucial filter, ensuring your roadmap prioritizes only what aligns with the movement.

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, founder mode, more | Anneka Gupta
insight: Prioritize making a decision with ~70% confidence, then iterate; waiting for perfect information leads to analysis paralysis and missed learning.
pull_quote: It's not about making the right decision, it's about making the decision. Operate off imprecise information, because once you commit, you learn more. Aim for 70% right, then iterate. No decision means no high-fidelity information.

episode_title: This will make you a better decision maker | Annie Duke (Thinking In Bets, former pro poker player)
insight: Actively shorten long feedback loops by identifying and tracking necessary, correlated short-term signals and intermediate outcomes.
pull_quote: There's no such thing as a long feedback loop; you choose its length. Shorten it by tracking necessary, correlated short-term signals and intermediate outcomes. Don't wait a decade for results when you can measure progress in months.

episode_title: Behind the scenes of Calendly’s rapid growth | Annie Pearl (CPO)
insight: Defining your precise target market and persona is the biggest unlock for efficient product prioritization and building an amazing product.
pull_quote: It's one of the harder things for companies to do. The ability to say no is going to allow you to build something amazing for the people that matter most, not something average for many. For example, we deprioritized a Venmo integration because our target market is teams, not solopreneurs.

episode_title: Microsoft CPO: If you aren’t prototyping with AI you’re doing it wrong | Aparna Chennapragada
insight: Leverage AI-powered prototyping and prompt sets to rapidly visualize ideas and accelerate the product building loop.
pull_quote: If you aren’t prototyping and building to see what you want to build, you’re doing it wrong. The prompt sets are the new PRDs, accelerating the product building loop with demos before memos.

episode_title: How to sell your ideas and rise within your company | Casey Winters, Eventbrite
insight: In uncertain, resource-constrained environments, PMs must make decisions, ship to learn, and apply critical thinking beyond frameworks.
pull_quote: Many product managers say they can't create solutions without seeing all the data and talking to customers. In reality, the fastest way to learn is often to ship, especially when resources are scarce. Use your brain; don't just follow a process.

episode_title: Why ChatGPT will be the next big growth channel (and how to capitalize on it) | Brian Balfour
insight: Embrace new distribution platforms early, as competitors will, and customer expectations will shift, making opting out impossible.
pull_quote: Don't trick yourself into thinking you can opt out of new platform shifts. It's a prisoner's dilemma: if you don't play, your competitors will, and customer expectations will change. You have to play the game.

episode_title: Brian Chesky’s new playbook
insight: True leadership involves being in the product details, not just delegating, to ensure quality and coherence across teams.
pull_quote: There's a difference between micromanagement and being in the details. How do you know people are doing a good job if you're not in the details? Leaders are in the details.

episode_title: Lessons from scaling Uber and Opendoor | Brian Tolkin (Head of Product at Opendoor, ex-Uber)
insight: In low-volume contexts, honestly assess A/B test feasibility, then build conviction with diverse methods or trust informed intuition.
pull_quote: In low-volume contexts, don't force A/B tests; run a power analysis and be honest about runtime. If not feasible, increase conviction by talking to customers, using observational data, or accepting longer runtimes. Sometimes, trust informed intuition.

episode_title: 'Inside Canva: Coaches not managers, giving away your Legos, and embracing AI | Cameron Adams'
insight: Overcome blank page fear in onboarding by guiding users through simple, low-effort steps that build to a surprising accomplishment and deep delight.
pull_quote: We learned to overcome the 'blank page fear' in onboarding. Guide users with simple steps, like 'search for a monkey,' to quickly build something they never thought they could. This surprising accomplishment drove immense delight and "I didn't know I could be a designer" moments.

episode_title: The things engineers are desperate for PMs to understand | Camille Fournier (“The Manager’s Path”)
insight: PMs hoarding ideas forces engineers to find creative outlets in over-engineering, which ultimately harms the product.
pull_quote: When product managers hoard all the ideas, engineers seek creative outlets in over-engineering. This is ultimately bad for the product. Involve your team in ideation to channel their creativity effectively.

episode_title: Monetizing passions, scaling marketplaces, and stories from a creator economy vet | Camille Hearst
insight: For marketplaces, prioritize solving supply-side pain points, as great demand means nothing without available product.
pull_quote: It doesn't matter how nice the user experience is, how much demand you can generate if when someone opens that app, there are no cars available. You can't lose sight of solving real pain points for the supply side to ensure the business operates.

episode_title: How Notion leveraged community to build a $10B business | Camille Ricketts
insight: Apply product-market fit thinking to content creation, identifying audience needs and crafting "painkiller" content.
pull_quote: The way that you think about product market fit, you have to think about content market fit. Identify your audience and what causes them anxiety. Create content that acts as a painkiller, addressing their real-life needs.

episode_title: How to achieve hypergrowth in your business and career | Carilu Dietrich (Atlassian)
insight: In early stages, build non-scalable processes like deep customer engagement to intimately understand user struggles for lasting solutions.
pull_quote: Gusto's founders personally ran payroll for their first 200 customers. Early-stage startups must embrace non-scalable processes to deeply understand customer struggles and build truly beloved products.

episode_title: How to build deeper, more robust relationships | Carole Robin (Stanford professor, “Touchy Feely”)
insight: Recognize anger as a secondary emotion, often masking fear or hurt; expressing these core feelings fosters deeper connection and problem-solving.
pull_quote: Anger is often a secondary emotion, typically masking fear or hurt. Expressing your underlying fear or hurt (or acknowledging others' fear) connects people and rallies teams faster than anger ever could.

episode_title: Why most product managers are unprepared for the demands of a real startup | Casey Winters
insight: Prepare for important meetings by anticipating every possible question and having all data ready to significantly reduce negative outcomes.
pull_quote: To prepare for important meetings, anticipate every possible question and have all data ready. This dramatically reduces the chance of a negative outcome.

episode_title: How to sell your ideas and rise within your company | Casey Winters, Eventbrite
insight: When facing disruptors with seemingly terrible business models, assume they are right and base your strategy on them playing optimally.
pull_quote: Grubhub assumed DoorDash's negative-margin model was stupid and unsustainable. When facing existential threats, overreact: assume the disruptor is right and base your strategy on their optimal game.

episode_title: An operator’s guide to product strategy | Chandra Janakiraman (CPO at VRChat, ex-Meta, Headspace)
insight: Prioritize strategic opportunities by assessing expected impact, certainty, clarity of levers, and unique differentiation your team offers.
pull_quote: Don't just pick opportunities; rank them by impact, confidence, solution clarity, and whether your team offers unique capabilities to truly differentiate your product.

episode_title: Mastering product strategy and growing as a PM | Maggie Crowley (Toast, Drift, TripAdvisor)
insight: Shift from merely meeting customer desires to identifying and fulfilling their unrecognized needs to truly define your product.
pull_quote: Airbnb's product wasn't home sharing, but 'belonging anywhere.' True product definition goes beyond desires to fulfilling unrecognized needs. Discover these fundamental needs to unlock deeper product-market fit and guide your entire strategy.

episode_title: Al Engineering 101 with Chip Huyen (Nvidia, Stanford, Netflix)
insight: To build successful AI products, focus on talking to users and improving fundamental processes, not just chasing the latest AI tech.
pull_quote: What people think improves AI apps: staying up to date with latest news, adopting new frameworks, agonizing over databases. What actually improves them: talking to users, preparing better data, optimizing workflows.

episode_title: Launching and growing a podcast | Chris Hutchins (All the Hacks, Wealthfront, Google)
insight: Focus on being someone's favorite, not everyone's 'okay,' to build a strong, advocated user base and achieve product-market fit.
pull_quote: Try to be someone's favorite. Don't try to be everyone's okay podcast. Every episode, except one, was someone's favorite. That's how you build true fans.

episode_title: The essence of product management | Christian Idiodi (SVPG)
insight: Achieve product-market fit by finding 6-8 B2B or 15-25 B2C reference customers who will stake their reputation on your solution.
pull_quote: Don't launch hoping it works. You're not ready until you have 6-8 B2B or 15-25 B2C reference customers. These are people who love your solution so much they'll stake their reputation on it, providing real product-market fit.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to OKRs | Christina Wodtke (Stanford)
insight: Streamline OKR approvals by having peer teams provide rapid feedback instead of relying on slow, hierarchical management approvals.
pull_quote: The OKR approval process can kill your velocity. Instead of slow boss approvals, get three trusted peer teams to review your OKRs in 24 hours. This fast peer feedback drives alignment without bottlenecks.

episode_title: Understanding the role of product ops | Christine Itwaru (Pendo)
insight: Product Ops should build essential systems, automate core processes, and then pivot to higher-level strategic value.
pull_quote: Product ops shouldn't just run processes. Build the necessary systems, then automate and get out of the way to focus on more strategic value, such as increasing retention or driving growth.

episode_title: How to become a category pirate | Christopher Lochhead (Author of Play Bigger, Niche Down, more)
insight: Competing in an existing market means fighting for 24% of the value; designing a new category allows you to capture two-thirds.
pull_quote: In tech categories, one company earns two-thirds (76%) of total value. Competing in existing markets means fighting for the remaining 24%. Design your own category to capture the majority.

episode_title: Relentless curiosity, radical accountability, and HubSpot’s winning growth formula | Chris Miller
insight: Take radical ownership of neglected opportunities and proactively fix them; it unlocks outsized growth and builds trust.
pull_quote: HubSpot's early growth team identified a neglected self-service revenue stream that "no one was committing any code to." They took ownership, redesigned it, and saw a step-function change in business physics, proving that radical accountability fuels growth.

episode_title: An inside look at Figma’s unique GTM motion | Claire Butler (first GTM hire)
insight: To succeed in 0-to-1, go to extreme, unscalable lengths to unblock and delight your very first users.
pull_quote: Their engineer couldn't open the file. Dylan drove our CTO to fix their MacBook just to get them to use the product. You must go to extreme, unscalable lengths to get your first users over the hump.

episode_title: Lessons from scaling Stripe | Claire Hughes Johnson (ex-COO of Stripe)
insight: Act as the decision-maker to maintain momentum, and if unsure, just ask, don't get stuck and slow things down.
pull_quote: If you're not sure who the decision maker is, it's probably you. Act that way to avoid slowing the company down. Don't get stuck – just ask, or decide, to drive progress, impact, and momentum.

episode_title: Bending the universe in your favor | Claire Vo (LaunchDarkly, Color, Optimizely, ChatPRD)
insight: Increase clock speed by moving deadlines one iteration faster; year-long tasks become half-year, half-year tasks become quarterly.
pull_quote: To increase pace, challenge your leaders to bring clock speed one click faster. If something needs to be done this year, target this half. If this half, target this quarter. This simple shift can transform organizational momentum.

episode_title: How to scrappily hire for, measure, and unlock growth | Crystal Widjaja, Gojek and Kumu
insight: Don't just track metrics; instrument events with properties to understand user context and generate actionable insights.
pull_quote: Power users do four times more bookings is an observation, not an insight. An insight tells you why, providing context like 'power users use free shipping on high GMV baskets,' guiding specific marketing changes.

episode_title: Lessons from 1,000+ YC startups: Resilience, tar pit ideas, pivoting, more | Dalton Caldwell (YC)
insight: Overcome social anxiety to regularly engage customers in person for true product understanding, not just through online methods.
pull_quote: Most founders hide behind keyboards. Don't. Overcome social anxiety and consistently talk to customers in person. It's the only way to genuinely understand them and your product.

episode_title: Developing a growth model + marketplace growth strategy | Dan Hockenmaier
insight: The biggest wins in retention come from inflecting the early user experience; homogenize first-time encounters to prove consistent value.
pull_quote: The biggest wins in retention often come from inflecting the early user experience, not from trying to resurrect churned users. Homogenize first-time encounters to teach and prove the product's value consistently.

episode_title: The AI-native startup: 5 products, 7-figure revenue, 100% AI-written code. | Dan Shipper (Every)
insight: Empower engineers to manage AI agents that write all code, shifting the product focus from manual coding to strategy and review.
pull_quote: No one is manually coding anymore. Organizations like ours, people who are playing at the edge, we're doing things that, in three years, everybody else is going to be doing.

episode_title: Daniel Lereya
insight: Spending more time developing a product does not yield better results; instead, set strict time-based release traps to focus and get fast customer feedback.
pull_quote: Spending more time on product development often creates more questions and complications, not better results. Set time-based traps to focus on core value and ship fast. Don't fear early, imperfect feedback; it's priceless.

episode_title: Building a culture of excellence | David Singleton (CTO of Stripe)
insight: To generate truly innovative names, abandon large brainstorms; use small, diverse teams with varied or disguised briefs to unlock fresh creative directions.
pull_quote: Ditch big-team brainstorms for naming. Our best names come from small, two-person teams, often given disguised briefs (e.g., naming a bicycle for an AI product) to foster breakthrough, unconventional ideas.

episode_title: Building a culture of excellence | David Singleton (CTO of Stripe)
insight: Implement 'friction logging' by role-playing specific user journeys to identify and prioritize critical pain points for meticulous craft investment.
pull_quote: Friction logging requires role-playing a specific user's journey through your product, meticulously noting every point of difficulty. This process reveals critical areas to invest in, driving impactful product improvements and revenue uplifts.

episode_title: Succeeding as an introvert, building zero-to-one, and PM’ing your career like a product | Deb Liu
insight: Treat your career as a product by defining success metrics, skills as features, and creating an intentional roadmap to achieve goals.
pull_quote: Some of the best PMs are terrible PMs for their career; they just drift from job to job. But if you wrote a spec for your career, what would success look like? How would you get there?

episode_title: How Block is becoming the most AI-native enterprise in the world | Dhanji R. Prasanna
insight: Non-technical people using AI agents to build software tools for themselves is driving the most surprising and impactful productivity gains.
pull_quote: The most surprising and amazing impact of AI is non-technical people using agents and programming tools to build things. They are showing the most impact by optimizing their particular workday and tasks.

episode_title: Zigging vs. zagging: How HubSpot built a $30B company | Dharmesh Shah (co-founder/CTO)
insight: Always factor in the long-term, third-order cost of dimensional complexity when adding a new product or feature.
pull_quote: When adding a new product or feature, don't just consider development and maintenance. Recognize the third-order cost of dimensional complexity: every future decision must be made through its lens, escalating overhead. This silent cost reliably kills companies.

episode_title: How Revolut trains world-class PMs: The “Local CEO” model, raw intellect & building wow products
insight: Recognize that a product 99% done is closer to 0% than 100%; relentless focus on full execution ensures real impact.
pull_quote: If something is 99% done, it's closer to 0% rather than 100%. Product owners must ensure full execution, from build to customer care, so features aren't useless.

episode_title: How to discover your superpowers, own your story, and unlock personal growth | Donna Lichaw
insight: Apply user research methods to leadership: gather feedback from your team to validate or debunk the stories you tell yourself about your impact.
pull_quote: A CEO's self-story was 'Nobody listens, I'm too nice.' User research with his team revealed they valued his caring, wanted vision, and empowered autonomy. This clarified true impact.

episode_title: The Godmother of AI on jobs, robots & why world models are next | Dr. Fei-Fei Li
insight: To build truly intelligent systems, prioritize gathering massive, well-structured datasets; they are often the critically-overlooked ingredient.
pull_quote: Early AI models lacked data. Our breakthrough with ImageNet showed that big data, neural networks, and GPUs were the golden recipe for modern AI advancements, enabling object recognition.

episode_title: Behind the founder | Drew Houston (Dropbox)
insight: At strategic inflection points, go all-in on one path rather than hedging, even if it feels insane, to avoid being a second-best product everywhere.
pull_quote: When facing existential threats, a company must go all-in on one strategic direction, like Intel exiting memory for microprocessors, rather than hedging and being second-best everywhere.

episode_title: Figma’s CEO: Why AI makes design, craft, and quality the new moat for startups | Dylan Field
insight: Intuition is a hypothesis generator; validate these hypotheses with data to form a working theory for product development.
pull_quote: Intuition is like a hypothesis generator. You constantly generate ideas, then debate them and find data to support or negate them, winnowing down to a working hypothesis.

episode_title: Dylan Field live at Figma's Config: Intuition, simplicity, and the future of design
insight: Dedicate resources to systematically fix user 'blockers' because it measurably improves retention and activation more than new features.
pull_quote: Removing user blockers is as important for retaining users as adding cool new stuff. Our 'Blockers' team struck them down one by one, seeing measurable improvements in retention and activation metrics with each fix.

episode_title: Crafting a compelling product vision | Ebi Atawodi (YouTube, Netflix, Uber)
insight: Continuously maintain a crisp, clear list of the top 3-10 problems users face to ground all product vision and strategy.
pull_quote: It baffles me how many PMs ramble when asked about the top problems that keep them up at night. Curate a living document of 3-10 crisp, clear problems to consistently ground your product vision and strategy.

episode_title: The $1B Al company training ChatGPT, Claude & Gemini on the path to responsible AGI | Edwin Chen
insight: Don't optimize your product for superficial engagement or flashiness; instead, build for truth, deep utility, and genuine user value.
pull_quote: We're teaching models to chase dopamine instead of truth. Current AI leaderboards reward crazy emojis, bolding, and length, even if models hallucinate. This optimizes for 'AI slop' over genuine advancement.

episode_title: How to foster innovation and big thinking | Eeke de Milliano (Retool, Stripe)
insight: To quickly launch new products, fund small teams like internal startups and keep them separate from the core product for independent, rapid iteration.
pull_quote: Retool launched three products in one year by funding small teams like internal startups, keeping them separate from the core product. This enabled rapid iteration and distinct customer focus.

episode_title: 10 growth tactics that never work | Elena Verna (Amplitude, Miro, Dropbox, SurveyMonkey)
insight: Growth teams must prioritize building owned, earned acquisition channels like virality or user-generated content for sustainable growth.
pull_quote: Growth teams must prioritize building owned, earned channels like virality or user-generated content. Don't rely solely on rented channels like SEO/SEM; algorithms can giveth and taketh away. Your cost of acquisition will only rise.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to product-led sales | Elena Verna
insight: In product-led sales, product must take accountability for customer acquisition and pipeline generation, not just building features.
pull_quote: Do not implement product-led sales in marketing; it's a recipe for disaster within six months. Product must take accountability for selling the product itself.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to product-led sales | Elena Verna
insight: Invest in building owned and earned acquisition channels like virality and user-generated content for sustainable long-term growth.
pull_quote: Your growth team's top priority should be building owned and earned acquisition channels. Reliance on SEO, SEM, and social makes Google richer and leaves you vulnerable to algorithms. Earned channels, like virality or UGC, are stable and defensible.

episode_title: The new AI growth playbook for 2026 | How Lovable hit $200M ARR in one year
insight: For new AI products, aggressively give away product access, tracking it as a marketing cost to accelerate adoption and "wow" moments.
pull_quote: Give your product away a lot. For AI products, remove the barrier of entry; give a lot away for free. We track it as marketing costs, not something to reduce to make our margins better. This is part of our growth secret sauce.

episode_title: Rethinking SEO in the age of AI | Eli Schwartz (SEO advisor, author)
insight: PMs must treat SEO as a product, deeply understanding the user's self-discovery journey to identify if and how search drives conversions.
pull_quote: Think of SEO as a product; PMs should lead by understanding the user's self-discovery journey. If users aren't searching for your solution, or your product requires a complex sales motion, SEO likely isn't your best investment.

episode_title: How Netflix builds a culture of excellence | Elizabeth Stone (CTO)
insight: Centralize data and insights, including user research, to foster objectivity, functional excellence, and holistic product problem-solving.
pull_quote: Netflix keeps its data, analytics, and user research teams centralized and functionally diverse. This structure fosters objectivity, deep functional expertise, and cross-pollination, making them truth-tellers for product decisions.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to PR | Emilie Gerber (founder of Six Eastern)
insight: When pitching press, frame your product as a better alternative to an existing solution, rather than claiming category creation.
pull_quote: For PR, 'category creator' is not a useful tactic. Instead, position your product as doing what a household name does, but better, to give reporters an instant frame of reference.

episode_title: How to build a powerful marketing machine | Emily Kramer (Asana, Carta, MKT1)
insight: Product-led growth is a misnomer; it truly means product plus robust marketing, requiring deep collaboration, not just product.
pull_quote: Product-led growth is a misnomer. It really means product plus marketing. You're going to have a bigger marketing team early on because sales isn't communicating with customers, so marketing fills that gap.

episode_title: How Intercom rose from the ashes by betting everything on AI | Eoghan McCabe (founder and CEO)
insight: Price AI products based on the value delivered, like resolved customer problems, rather than cost, anticipating future efficiency gains.
pull_quote: We charged 99 cents per resolved customer ticket, even though it initially cost us $1.20. Our pricing aligned 100% with attained value, betting on future efficiency and customer preference for predictable, fair rates.

episode_title: Countdown of the top 10 episodes of the year
insight: Categorize tasks as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead to focus effort on high-impact work and avoid burnout.
pull_quote: My to-do list was endless. Not all tasks are created equal: some give 100x impact (leverage), others just 1x (neutral), or less (overhead). Focus your best energy on leverage tasks.

episode_title: Reflections on a movement | Eric Ries (creator of the Lean Startup methodology)
insight: Quality is customer-defined; without knowing your customer, you don't truly understand what 'quality' means.
pull_quote: Quality is defined by the customer. If we don't know who our customer is, we literally don't know what 'quality' means. Building a high quality product with the wrong value proposition is nothing to be proud of.

episode_title: Inside Bolt: From near-death to one of the fastest-growing products in history | Eric Simons
insight: Leverage interactive, high-fidelity working prototypes to articulate product vision and features more effectively and quickly than static documentation.
pull_quote: It's now faster to make real prototypes with working software than dragging frames in Figma. If a picture is worth a thousand words, a live actual demo is worth millions; you can feel it, it's real.

episode_title: Taking control of your career | Ethan Evans (Amazon)
insight: Systematic invention requires dedicated, regular time for focused thinking, primarily by combining existing concepts.
pull_quote: People think invention takes all this time, but you only need two hours once a month. Dedicate time to combine existing concepts; you don't need many good ideas to be seen as tremendously inventive.

episode_title: Taking control of your career | Ethan Evans (Amazon)
insight: Allocate dedicated time to systematically combine existing ideas and focus on continuous optimization; true invention is rarely pure magic.
pull_quote: People think invention takes all this time, but you only need two hours once a month. The thing is once you have one good idea, it often takes years to express that. Jeff and Amazon had ideas like Prime shipping; it's a 20-year-old idea still getting better.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to AEO: How to get ChatGPT to recommend your product | Ethan Smith (Graphite)
insight: Early-stage companies can quickly win high-converting leads through Answer Engine Optimization, a channel where traditional SEO falls short.
pull_quote: Webflow saw 6X higher conversion rates from LLM traffic vs. Google Search. Unlike traditional SEO, early-stage companies can quickly win these valuable leads via Answer Engine Optimization (AEO) by getting frequently cited.

episode_title: Improve strategy, influence, and decision-making by understanding your brain | Evan LaPointe
insight: Understand your brain's biases, like low openness to abstract ideas, to actively seek diverse perspectives for strong product vision.
pull_quote: Your brain might be wired to resist abstract vision work if you have low openness. Be self-aware, understand this bias, and actively seek diverse perspectives to build a stronger product vision.

episode_title: Failure
insight: Product execution cannot overcome a fundamentally broken business model or underlying 'math problem' that prevents viable returns.
pull_quote: A company is a math problem: if the foundational equation is broken, no amount of product execution can get you out of the failed outputs. Quibi's multi-billion dollar bet on bespoke content was an unfixable math problem.

episode_title: How to build trust and grow as a product leader | Fareed Mosavat (Reforge, Slack, Instacart, Pixar)
insight: Leaders must define necessary resources for impact, not just work within current constraints, to truly own business outcomes.
pull_quote: When you're a leader, it's your job to propose, 'What are the resources we need to have the most impact on this problem?' Don't just work with what you have. Marshal resources across the organization.

episode_title: How Shopify builds a high-intensity culture | Farhan Thawar (VP and Head of Eng)
insight: Prioritize building foundational platforms that enable future features to be built in hours, rather than point solutions taking weeks.
pull_quote: Instead of building a specific feature in weeks, focus on creating a platform layer so anyone can build that feature in one hour. This puts ‘gas in the tank’ for long-term growth and unknown future use cases.

episode_title: The Godmother of AI on jobs, robots & why world models are next | Dr. Fei-Fei Li
insight: To truly advance AI, models needed massive, real-world data, mirroring how humans learn from constant experience.
pull_quote: For decades, AI models were stuck due to lack of data. Just like humans learn from massive, constant experience, machines needed millions of labeled examples to truly learn object recognition.

episode_title: Inside the expert network training every frontier AI model | Garrett Lord
insight: Launch internal ventures successfully by empowering a separate, founder-led, metrics-focused team, unburdened by existing priorities.
pull_quote: I focused 80% of my time. We separated everything: engineering, design, ops, finance. Everyone had one job only: making Handshake AI successful. That was absolutely critical.

episode_title: Mastering onboarding | Lauryn Isford (Head of Growth at Airtable)
insight: Maintain a "secret roadmap" for breakthrough ideas users don't ask for, to truly revolutionize behavior and gain a competitive edge.
pull_quote: Besides a public roadmap of user requests, maintain a 'secret roadmap' for disruptive ideas no one asked for. These hidden projects, born from unique insights, truly change user behavior and deliver breakthrough wins.

episode_title: Velocity over everything: How Ramp became the fastest-growing SaaS startup ever | Geoff Charles
insight: Prioritize velocity by reducing planning accuracy for most features, dedicating that time to execution instead.
pull_quote: Any second you spend planning is a second you don't spend doing. Don't waste time on planning accuracy for most features; rather, spend that time creating velocity.

episode_title: Geoffrey Moore on finding your beachhead, crossing the chasm, and dominating a market
insight: When crossing the chasm, focus your pitch entirely on the customer's severe, unmet problem, not your product's features or vision.
pull_quote: When engaging pragmatist customers, shut your laptop. Don't talk about your product or vision. Focus entirely on *their* serious problem; they need therapy, not a demo, and will talk if you listen intently.

episode_title: Leaving big tech to build the #1 technology newsletter | Gergely Orosz (The Pragmatic Engineer)
insight: When launching a new product, observe and "double down on pulls"—strong, unexpected surges of user interest or demand.
pull_quote: Gergely's initial book plan pivoted when a blog post draft unexpectedly generated 20 DMs/hour asking to read it. He realized the "pull" of interest meant turning it into an ebook.

episode_title: Customer-led growth | Georgiana Laudi (Forget The Funnel)
insight: Focusing on ideal customer segments and updating messaging can significantly increase website and trial conversion, even without product changes.
pull_quote: Refocusing on the ideal customer and updating website messaging increased one company's website conversion by 89% and trial-to-paid by 40%, without touching the product.

episode_title: 35 years of product design wisdom from Apple, Disney, Pinterest and beyond | Bob Baxley
insight: Align leaders on Growth, Engagement, and Monetization (GEM) priorities to eliminate fundamental misalignment.
pull_quote: The number one source of misalignment I discover among startups is not agreeing on Growth, Engagement, and Monetization. Get leaders aligned on how to prioritize these basic factors.

episode_title: Scaling Duolingo, embracing failure, and insight into Latin America’s tech scene | Gina Gotthilf
insight: Cultivating a unique, quirky brand voice evokes emotion, drives memorability, and fosters strong organic word-of-mouth.
pull_quote: The way we talk to you is a little bit funny. It doesn't take ourselves too seriously and it makes the person receiving this message feel something. Use that to your benefit.

episode_title: Gokul Rajaram on designing your product development process, when and how to hire your first PM, a playbook for hiring leaders, getting ahead in your career, how to get started angel investing, and more
insight: Avoid becoming a feature factory by empowering teams to solve customer problems and measure impact, rather than just shipping features.
pull_quote: A feature factory ships features without knowing their impact on customer behavior. Empower product teams to solve problems, measure results, and focus on outcomes, not just outputs.

episode_title: How to break out of autopilot and create the life you want | Graham Weaver (Stanford GSB professor)
insight: Consistently writing down your product goal and three daily actions accelerates progress more in three months than in three years.
pull_quote: Writing down your core product goal and three daily actions toward it helps you accomplish more in three months than in three years without it. This simple, consistent habit is incredibly powerful.

episode_title: “Dumbest idea I’ve heard” to $100M ARR: Inside the rise of Gamma | Grant Lee (co-founder)
insight: To achieve true product-market fit, make the first 30 seconds of user onboarding magical and shareable by focusing on one core value.
pull_quote: We won Product Hunt but lacked word-of-mouth. True PMF arrived only after we revamped onboarding to make the first 30 seconds magical, driving organic viral growth. We didn't need ads.

episode_title: Everyone’s an engineer now: Inside v0’s mission to create 100 million builders | Guillermo Rauch
insight: AI empowers product managers to directly build, prototype, and spec out live products, transforming PRDs into interactive artifacts.
pull_quote: Some of the best things created with v0 come from the product management team. They're now actually building the product, speccing out new functionality as a live PRD. Just ship it.

episode_title: Lessons from working with 600+ YC startups | Gustaf Alströmer (Y Combinator, Airbnb)
insight: Companies fail when they don't talk to users, preventing product-market fit, which makes all other efforts irrelevant.
pull_quote: If I drill down what makes companies fail, it's quite simple. They don't talk to users, which means they don't find product market fit. If they don't find product market fit, nothing else really matters.

episode_title: The science of product, big bets, and how AI is impacting the future of music | Gustav Söderström
insight: When redesigning a core product experience, protect existing user recall behaviors; don't replace current high-intent actions with discovery features.
pull_quote: Spotify's home screen was 90% recall. Our redesign shifted it to 10% recall, 90% discovery. Users complained, "I can't find my playlists anymore!" Don't sacrifice essential user recall for new discovery features.

episode_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar
insight: Appoint a single, trusted domain expert, often the PM, as a 'benevolent dictator' for AI error analysis to ensure tractability.
pull_quote: To keep AI error analysis tractable, appoint one "benevolent dictator" whose taste you trust. This person, often the Product Manager, needs domain expertise to simplify the process and cut through noise.

episode_title: Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders | Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar
insight: Before writing AI product tests, always perform qualitative error analysis on user data to identify actual problems.
pull_quote: Many teams make the common trap of jumping straight to AI product tests. Start with data analysis to ground what you should test, identifying actual errors from user interactions first.

episode_title: Business strategy with Hamilton Helmer (author of 7 Powers)
insight: While operational excellence is crucial for achieving competitive position, it rarely constitutes a durable source of power because it can often be mimicked.
pull_quote: Operational excellence is essential for attaining competitive position, but it's not typically a source of power. If competitors can mimic your processes, you're on a treadmill.

episode_title: LinkedIn’s product evolution and the art of building complex systems | Hari Srinivasan (LinkedIn)
insight: Implement structured decision-making processes, like clear ownership and escalation rules, to rapidly resolve blockers in complex product ecosystems.
pull_quote: In complex product ecosystems, clear decision-making mechanisms are crucial. LinkedIn uses a 'five-day escalation rule' to unblock issues and assigns a single 'decision-maker' (RAPID) for rapid clarity and progress.

episode_title: The art and wisdom of changing teams | Heidi Helfand (Author of Dynamic Reteaming)
insight: To foster innovation, create isolated teams with process freedom, clear decision-makers, and protection from distractions.
pull_quote: For innovation, create isolated teams with process freedom. Ensure they report to a clear decision-maker, and crucially, protect them from external distractions and bureaucracy. This setup saved Expertcity with GoToMyPC.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to adding a PLG motion | Hila Qu (Reforge, GitLab)
insight: Product-led growth is fundamentally data-led growth; without analyzing free user behavior, your free product yields no strategic value.
pull_quote: When offering a free product, you gain broader reach and critical usage data. Without analyzing this data, giving away your product is for nothing. PLG is fundamentally 'data-led growth.'

episode_title: How to build a team that can “take a punch” | Hilary Gridley (Head of Core Product, Whoop)
insight: When criticized, counter negative narratives by taking a small action that demonstrates the opposite of what others might think.
pull_quote: Don't litigate others' impressions of you. Instead, take a small, specific action that demonstrates the opposite of what you fear they think. This counter-programs the narrative and helps you move forward.

episode_title: How we restructured Airtable's entire org for AI | Howie Liu (co-founder and CEO)
insight: For an existing company adapting to AI, ask if your mission is better served by re-founding with an AI-native approach.
pull_quote: If you were founding a new company today with the same mission, how would you execute it using a fully AI-native approach? If you can't, consider selling and starting fresh to truly embrace the mission.

episode_title: What it takes to become a top 1% PM | Ian McAllister (Uber, Amazon, Airbnb)
insight: The biggest mistake in working backwards is starting with a solution and then retrofitting a problem, instead of truly obsessing over the problem first.
pull_quote: When teams "work backwards" incorrectly, they start with a solution then retrofit a problem. The key is to genuinely obsess about the customer's problem first, being guided by it before ever developing a solution.

episode_title: The future of AI in software development | Inbal Shani (CPO of GitHub)
insight: Don't chase AI for AI's sake; identify customer problems first, then leverage AI to build impactful solutions.
pull_quote: Many companies rush to adopt AI without a clear purpose. Instead, work backward from the customer problem, then leverage AI to create valuable solutions.

episode_title: I’ve run 75+ businesses. Here’s why you’re probably chasing the wrong idea. | Andrew Wilkinson
insight: Ask Eigenquestions to simplify complex problems and make decisive progress, even with minimal information.
pull_quote: When facing a new, ambiguous problem, identify the one or two 'Eigenquestions' – the critical questions that, once answered, will determine your entire strategy. For example, with a teleportation device: Is it safe for humans? Is it expensive to buy or run? These answers define your product's market.

episode_title: Becoming evidence-guided | Itamar Gilad (Gmail, YouTube, Microsoft)
insight: Validate product ideas with no-code fake door tests and usability studies before writing a single line of code to save time and resources.
pull_quote: For Gmail's tabbed inbox, we showed HTML facades and manually sorted emails in tests. No code was written, but it gave us evidence to build. Start with fake door tests to validate cheaply.

episode_title: Notion's lost years, near collapse during COVID, staying small to move fast, building horizontal
insight: Hide your ambitious vision as a 'sugar-coated broccoli' inside a widely desired product form factor to achieve mass adoption.
pull_quote: People don't want to eat the broccoli, but people like sugar. Give them the sugar, then hide your vision inside. This 'sugar-coated broccoli' approach enables mass adoption of complex ideas.

episode_title: Bending the universe in your favor | Claire Vo (LaunchDarkly, Color, Optimizely, ChatPRD)
insight: To grow your PM career, proactively ask your manager: "What should I work on now to be ready for [next role] later?"
pull_quote: To advance your PM career, proactively ask your manager, "What should I work on now to be ready for [next role] later?" This approach gets them on your side, focuses their feedback, and sets a clear path for growth.

episode_title: 'Behind the product: Duolingo streaks | Jackson Shuttleworth (Group PM, Retention Team)'
insight: Implement continuous experimentation, like 600 A/B tests in four years, to unlock massive, incremental product improvements.
pull_quote: We've run over 600 experiments on streaks in four years, effectively testing every other day. These constant, small A/B tests led to massive wins, proving relentless iteration drives product success.

episode_title: Be fundamentally different, not incrementally better | Jag Duggal (Nubank, Facebook, Google)
insight: Validate product-market fit with the Sean Ellis score, only scaling if 50% of users would be "very disappointed" without it.
pull_quote: Nubank uses the Sean Ellis score to determine product-market fit. They only scale a product if 50% of Brazilians (adjusting for culture) would be "very disappointed" if it went away. This high bar ensures customer fanaticism and prevents scaling a "dead cat."

episode_title: Making time for what matters | Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky (Authors of Make Time, Character VC)
insight: Trust your intuition to prioritize high-leverage work that might not be on formal roadmaps or OKRs to drive real impact.
pull_quote: You have great intuition about the highest leverage work you should do, even if it's not on your OKRs or boss's radar. Create space for it. You'll feel better, be more satisfied, and good things will happen.

episode_title: Making time for what matters | Jake Knapp and John Zeratsky (Authors of Make Time, Character VC)
insight: Invest 10 hours in a Foundation Sprint to deeply align your team on a unique customer problem and differentiated approach before building.
pull_quote: Going fast can actually slow you down in the long run. Invest just 10 hours in a Foundation Sprint. It's the highest ROI time your team will spend, clarifying your unique customer promise and differentiation before you write a single line of code.

episode_title: Building better roadmaps | Janna Bastow (Mind the Product, ProdPad)
insight: The value of a roadmap lies in the roadmapping process, not the static document, by continuously checking strategic assumptions.
pull_quote: The value isn't in your roadmap, the value is in the roadmapping process. What you're actually doing is laying out your assumptions of the problems you're solving. Share early assumptions with your team and customers to check if you're on the right path.

episode_title: Scale AI CEO on Meta's $14B deal, scaling Uber Eats to $80B, & what frontier labs are building next
insight: Building a valuable product isn't enough; it must address the customer's most urgent daily problem to ensure successful adoption and market traction.
pull_quote: The biggest thing people miss when building new products is the urgency of the buyer. If your product provides a lot of value but isn't the top thing your customer thinks about daily, you're on a long road to a small town.

episode_title: How to get press for your product | Jason Feifer (editor in chief of Entrepreneur magazine)
insight: To get press, understand a publication's audience and mission, then frame your story to serve their readers, not just your product.
pull_quote: The editor, the writer... They don't care about you. They care about their reader or listener. Approach them with an understanding of what they're trying to do for their audience and how you can fit into that. If you don't, they are not interested in you.

episode_title: Jason Fried challenges your thinking on fundraising, goals, growth, and more
insight: Instead of estimating work duration, define a fixed 'appetite' (e.g., six weeks) as the budget you're willing to spend.
pull_quote: Don't estimate project time. Define a fixed 'appetite' – a maximum budget of time you're willing to spend on a feature. This radically shifts how you scope work and prevents projects from expanding indefinitely.

episode_title: We replaced our sales team with 20 AI agents—here’s what happened next | Jason Lemkin (SaaStr)
insight: Do not hire a VP of Sales until you have at least two individual sales reps consistently hitting their quota.
pull_quote: You need two sales reps hitting quota closing deals before you're ready to hire a manager for them. If you hire it before then, it never, ever works.

episode_title: Building a meaningful career | Jason Shah (Airbnb, Amazon, Microsoft, Alchemy)
insight: Reframe 'pushback' as aligning on shared goals to help the business succeed, fostering collaboration over conflict.
pull_quote: Forget 'pushback'; it's negative. Instead, identify the actual goal behind a request. Reframe your solution to align with that shared goal, turning potential disagreement into a clear path for business success.

episode_title: What world-class GTM looks like in 2026 | Jeanne DeWitt Grosser (Vercel, Stripe, Google)
insight: Use AI agents to analyze sales interactions and uncover true reasons for lost deals, providing deep, unbiased customer understanding.
pull_quote: The biggest loss that quarter according to the account executive was lost on price. When you ran the agent... it said actually you lost because you never really got in touch with an economic buyer.

episode_title: Building product at Stripe: craft, metrics, and customer obsession | Jeff Weinstein (Product lead)
insight: True product-market fit is proven by customers actually paying, not just expressing willingness to pay or interest.
pull_quote: Ready to pay is not the same as paying. Paying indicates a burning problem, an exchange of value for a promise. It's real commitment, unlike just saying they would pay.

episode_title: The paths to power: How to grow your influence and advance your career | Jeffrey Pfeffer (Stanford)
insight: Stop letting discomfort with power hinder your career and ability to influence product outcomes.
pull_quote: The biggest barrier to influence is often your own discomfort with power. Jeffrey Pfeffer notes: "If I think power is dirty, the first thing that's going to happen is I'm not going to do what I need to do to be successful in my career."

episode_title: The ultimate guide to founder-led sales | Jen Abel (co-founder of JJELLYFISH)
insight: Many early-stage B2B SaaS companies should sell time-boxed services first to educate buyers and earn the right to sell technology.
pull_quote: 40-50% of B2B SaaS companies sell services before technology. This educates buyers, secures logos, and sets the stage for product adoption, especially in traditional markets. Time-box services to 90-day increments.

episode_title: $1M to $10M: The enterprise sales playbook with Jen Abel
insight: To enter the enterprise market, sell services powered by your product first, then transition customers to the self-serve tool.
pull_quote: Enterprise customers consistently buy services, making them the fastest way to get your foot in the door. Sell your product as a service initially, then guide clients to leverage the underlying technology.

episode_title: Moving fast and navigating uncertainty | Jeremy Henrickson (Rippling, Coinbase)
insight: Avoid traditional MVPs; instead, design for the most complex use cases upfront to build a scalable product foundation that saves time long-term.
pull_quote: MVPs often lead to building the wrong technical foundation. Instead, design for your most complex use cases first to ensure long-term scalability and avoid costly re-architecture later.

episode_title: How have I been complicit in creating the conditions I say I don’t want? | Jerry Colonna
insight: Ask yourself how you've been complicit in creating undesirable conditions to evoke agency and change your reality.
pull_quote: The question I often ask is how have I been complicit in creating the conditions I say I don't want. The purpose of this question is to evoke your own agency, looking at ways you may have been diluting yourself.

episode_title: Building a world-class data org | Jessica Lachs (VP of Analytics and Data Science at DoorDash)
insight: Actively seek out and set concrete goals to eliminate rare, negative "fail states" as they drive churn and incur high costs.
pull_quote: Don't just track averages; actively seek and set concrete goals to eliminate rare "fail states." These terrible experiences, though infrequent, directly cause churn and are incredibly costly.

episode_title: How to see like a designer: The hidden power of typography and logos | Jessica Hische
insight: For early-stage products, prioritize the core experience over heavy brand investment; a pivot can make early brand spend obsolete.
pull_quote: If you invest super heavily on the whole brand vision from the jump, sometimes it's like throwing away money if you have to pivot. For many products, the brand should be somewhat invisible so the product itself becomes the star.

episode_title: The social radar: Y Combinator’s secret weapon | Jessica Livingston (co-founder of YC, author)
insight: Always be open-minded and avoid defensiveness when building products; adaptability to user feedback is key for success.
pull_quote: If a founder would get defensive, that was always a bad sign. The best founders are open-minded, ready to adjust, and eager to learn from feedback, not just defend initial ideas.

episode_title: Building minimum lovable products, stories from WeWork & Airbnb, and thriving as a PM | Jiaona Zhang
insight: Define ambitious OKRs by qualitatively outlining what 'crushing it' means, embracing red/yellow outcomes as learning.
pull_quote: Don't sandbag OKRs; define qualitatively what 'crushing it' truly means for users and the business. It's better to have red/yellow OKRs and learn why, than all green without impactful outcomes.

episode_title: How embracing your emotions will accelerate your career | Joe Hudson (Art of Accomplishment)
insight: Avoiding a difficult emotion in your work invites more of that very emotion into your professional life.
pull_quote: Whatever emotion you're trying to avoid, you are inviting into your life exactly as you're trying to avoid it. A conflict-avoidant CEO eventually deals with an entire organization full of unresolved tension and conflict.

episode_title: What differentiates the highest-performing product teams | John Cutler (The Beautiful Mess)
insight: Highest-performing product teams ensure deep coherence between their organizational structure and current strategy.
pull_quote: Brilliant product teams often fail when company structure doesn't align with strategy. No amount of empowerment can fix this core mismatch. Ensure your organizational design supports your strategic goals for true high performance.

episode_title: Conscious leadership: Unlocking vision, strategy and purpose | JM Nickels (Uber, Waymo, DoorDash)
insight: Prioritize creating an excellent product over optics or external approval to achieve superior outcomes and career growth.
pull_quote: The more I focused on optics and approval, the less I got promoted. By dropping the egoic, self-centered focus and building a fucking awesome product, I actually got recognized.

episode_title: The crazy story of landing Uber as a client | Jonathan Becker (Thrive Digital)
insight: Creative testing is the primary lever in modern performance marketing, demanding rigorous, data-driven iteration over automated bid optimization.
pull_quote: Automation shifted the paid growth lever from bids to creatives. Rigorous, data-driven creative testing is now paramount; ditch homogenous messaging for iterative, audience-specific assets across the funnel.

episode_title: How a great founder becomes a great CEO | Jonathan Lowenhar (co-founder of Enjoy The Work)
insight: Seduce potential buyers by crafting a future fantasy of value, focusing on what your product could become for them, not just past performance.
pull_quote: The best outcomes for early-stage startups happen when you're never for sale. You've seduced a buyer; they see the fantasy and fall in love with what your company could become for them. Focus on future value, not past metrics.

episode_title: Managing nerves, anxiety, and burnout | Jonny Miller (Nervous Systems Mastery)
insight: Emotions are crucial data for better decision-making, providing a competitive advantage for product leaders in complex environments.
pull_quote: The best decision-making is emotional. Patients unable to feel emotions struggle with basic choices, losing crucial intuitive data. Embrace your emotional sense for clear decisions, unskewed by fear.

episode_title: Competing with giants: An inside look at how The Browser Company builds product | Josh Miller (CEO)
insight: Prioritize optimizing for specific human feelings like joy or focus in product development, using metrics only to validate if those feelings are achieved.
pull_quote: Optimizing for numbers leaves a lot on the table and misses a lot. We talk about optimizing feelings: How do we want to make someone feel on the other end of our software?

episode_title: The UX Research reckoning is here | Judd Antin (Airbnb, Meta)
insight: Good micro-level user research doesn't slow development; it delivers immense business value rapidly, even in 48 hours.
pull_quote: The trope that "research slows us down" is false. Micro-level usability research can deliver multi-million dollar business impact in as little as 48 hours.

episode_title: Leveraging mentors to uplevel your career | Jules Walter (YouTube, Slack)
insight: To get more valuable feedback, always respond with enthusiastic gratitude, even if it's hard to hear, to encourage future sharing.
pull_quote: Most people don't focus on enthusiastic gratitude for feedback. But internally, even when your heart is melting, if you respond with genuine thanks, people will keep giving you valuable insights to help you grow.

episode_title: M&A, competition, pricing, and investing | Julia Schottenstein (dbt Labs)
insight: Before building, spend years as a consulting firm to manually solve problems and deeply understand user pain points firsthand.
pull_quote: dbt Labs started as a consulting firm for two years. They manually solved client problems, building dbt into the workflow. This hands-on approach directly informed the product, ensuring it tackled real pain points.

episode_title: From managing people to managing AI: The leadership skills everyone needs now | Julie Zhuo
insight: Embrace initial bad ideas as a necessary step to unlock truly novel and intuitive solutions, rather than walking away too soon.
pull_quote: Good ideas arrive after bad ideas are empty. Your brain identifies bad elements, then pattern matches novel ideas with greater intuition. Most creators resist bad ideas and never finish the creative process.

episode_title: From managing people to managing AI: The leadership skills everyone needs now | Julie Zhuo
insight: Use data to diagnose problems and discover opportunities, but apply creativity and design to invent the right solutions.
pull_quote: Diagnose with data and treat with design. Data helps you understand reality and where opportunities are, but it won't tell you what to build or how to solve problems.

episode_title: How To Win Friends & Influence Decisions (Julie Zhuo) | Lenny & Friends Summit 2024
insight: Diagnose product problems using data to understand reality, then apply creative design solutions, rather than relying solely on instinct.
pull_quote: Diagnose with data and treat with design. Data helps you figure out what's actually happening, but it won't tell you what to build. Amazing designers are obsessed with understanding reality through data.

episode_title: OpenAI researcher on why soft skills are the future of work | Karina Nguyen
insight: As AI excels at hard skills, product teams must master soft skills like prioritization, communication, empathy, and collaboration to drive innovation.
pull_quote: AI struggles with aesthetics, visual design, and extreme creativity. Prioritization, communication, management, empathy, and collaboration are the soft skills PMs must master as models automate hard tasks.

episode_title: Inside Linear: Building with taste, craft, and focus | Karri Saarinen (co-founder, designer, CEO)
insight: Relentlessly prioritize by asking if an activity directly helps customers and the core product ("main quest"), or is a distracting "side quest."
pull_quote: Avoid side quests. Always ask if an activity directly progresses the main quest line: building the product and making it awesome for customers. If not, it can be ignored or done later.

episode_title: Building beautiful products with Stripe’s Head of Design | Katie Dill (Stripe, Airbnb, Lyft)
insight: Implement a 'walk the store' process where multidisciplinary leaders regularly friction log critical user journeys to identify and prioritize quality fixes.
pull_quote: Products regress over time. Multidisciplinary leaders must regularly 'walk the store' through critical user journeys. Friction log the experience and calibrate quality scores across teams. This increases awareness, accountability, and informs roadmap prioritization.

episode_title: Twitter’s ex-Head of Product on Elon, consumer products, culture, more | Kayvon Beykpour
insight: Staff risky, speculative projects with people obsessed with the idea, ensuring they have the drive to will it into existence.
pull_quote: Staff projects with people obsessed with the idea they want to pursue. This passion makes them work harder, be more creative, and will the thing into existence, especially for speculative bets.

episode_title: An inside look at X’s Community Notes | Keith Coleman & Jay Baxter
insight: Small, self-selected teams with a clear founder, senior decision-maker, 100% focus, and dynamic goals accelerate impactful 0-to-1 building.
pull_quote: Community Notes credits its success to a "Thermal" team structure: one founder, one senior decision-maker, 100% focus, and dynamic goals. This setup enables rapid iteration and massive impact.

episode_title: Leading with empathy | Keith Yandell (DoorDash, Uber)
insight: For BD initiatives, validate demand with low-cost operational tests before investing product resources into scalable platform solutions.
pull_quote: Don't waste product cycles on BD initiatives. First, test your thesis with hacky operations to validate demand. Only then invest product resources into building scalable platform solutions, not bespoke ones.

episode_title: How to unlock your product leadership skills | Ken Norton, Ex-Google
insight: For senior product leaders, challenges often stem from people, not product; invest equally in communication and collaboration skills.
pull_quote: The number one category of challenges for senior product executives comes down to people, not product. Invest in communication and collaboration skills; they are as important, if not more, than technical product skills.

episode_title: Why not asking for what you want is holding you back | Kenneth Berger (exec coach, first PM @Slack)
insight: Seek 'hell yes' commitment from stakeholders, not lukewarm agreement, to ensure full alignment and successful execution.
pull_quote: Anything less than a 'hell yes' is a no. Don't settle for lukewarm agreement; ask 'What would it take for us to get to a hell yes?' to ensure true commitment.

episode_title: Taxi mafias, cash vaults & 100% MoM growth: The story of SEA’s biggest startup | Kevin Aluwi (Gojek)
insight: Doing the hard, operational things that competitors avoid builds deep customer loyalty and creates a durable, non-replicable competitive moat.
pull_quote: Gojek hired private security to protect drivers from taxi mafias. Doing these hard, operational things creates a durable moat because they build trust and commitment that competitors can't easily replicate.

episode_title: OpenAI’s CPO on how AI changes must-have skills, moats, coding, startup playbooks, more | Kevin Weil
insight: Build AI products at the edge of current model capabilities, anticipating rapid improvements to transform barely working ideas into successful ones.
pull_quote: If your product is at the edge of current AI capabilities, keep building. In a few months, models will be great, and your product that barely worked will sing. This "model maximalism" mindset leverages rapid AI advancements for future success.

episode_title: Unorthodox PM tips: Automating user insights, unselling candidates, decision logs, more | Kevin Yien
insight: Build product sense by consistently logging decisions, their rationale, and later reviewing actual outcomes to learn from your choices.
pull_quote: Improve product sense by getting more reps: Consistently log your decisions, their rationale, and crucially, see their outcomes. This free practice builds your decision-making muscle over time.

episode_title: Inside Devin: The AI engineer that's set to write 50% of its company’s code this year | Scott Wu
insight: The single most important step to build better relationships and give effective feedback is consistently soliciting criticism first.
pull_quote: If you say, 'Do you have any feedback for me?' You're wasting your breath. Ask instead: 'What could I do or stop doing that would make it easier to work with me?' And never accept 'no' as an answer.

episode_title: Using behavioral science to improve your product | Kristen Berman (Irrational Labs)
insight: Adding friction, like benefit-focused questions, to signup flows can increase conversion by engaging users with immediate value.
pull_quote: Counterintuitively, adding friction to a signup flow can increase conversion. Asking benefit-focused questions makes users engage more, like Trunk Club's 133% increase or TytoCare's 53% purchase uplift from a quiz.

episode_title: Growth tactics from OpenAI and Stripe’s first marketer | Krithika Shankarraman
insight: To avoid copying others, apply the DATE framework: Diagnose problems, Analyze competitors, Take a different path, and Experiment.
pull_quote: Most companies just copy others' successful tactics without understanding the inputs. Use the DATE framework—Diagnose, Analyze, Take a Different Path, Experiment—to build a truly differentiated GTM strategy.

episode_title: Kunal Shah on winning in India, second-order thinking, the philosophy of startups, and more
insight: In low-trust markets, a narrow product focus is a curse; successful companies build broad offerings around concentrated brand trust.
pull_quote: In low-trust markets, focus is a curse. Lack of trust creates concentration around powerful brands. Customers are wary of trying new things, so embrace broad offerings like 'super apps'.

episode_title: What sets great teams apart | Lane Shackleton (CPO of Coda)
insight: Use a structured feedback system like 'flash tags' (FYI, suggestion, recommendation, plea) to calibrate feedback intensity and improve decision velocity.
pull_quote: Feedback can slow everything down. Use 'flash tags' (FYI, suggestion, recommendation, plea) to calibrate the intensity of feedback. If there's one 'plea' among 100 FYIs, you know where to focus your attention.

episode_title: Career frameworks, A/B testing, onboarding tips, selling to engineers | Laura Schaffer (Amplitude)
insight: Prioritize existing customer retention over new acquisition, even if it means pausing all growth, to build long-term trust and brand loyalty.
pull_quote: During a supply crisis, we turned off our site for six months, sacrificing immediate growth. This ensured we served existing subscribers reliably, making us the only formula company to never run out and building undeniable long-term trust.

episode_title: Career frameworks, A/B testing, onboarding tips, selling to engineers | Laura Schaffer (Amplitude)
insight: Strategically lowering experiment confidence intervals, coupled with qualitative validation, can double experiment velocity and lead to more net successes over time.
pull_quote: Don't rigidly stick to a 95% confidence rate for product experiments. Strategically lowering the confidence interval can double your experiment velocity and lead to significantly more net successes over a year, when paired with qualitative data.

episode_title: Lessons from one of the world’s top executive recruiters | Lauren Ipsen (Daversa Partners, GC)
insight: For early-stage product leadership, prioritize hands-on builder DNA over big brand names from large, established companies.
pull_quote: The biggest mistake is being distracted by shiny objects and huge names. Those individuals are often far from the work. Look for someone closer to the work, who can operate like a PM while also building leadership.

episode_title: Mastering onboarding | Lauryn Isford (Head of Growth at Airtable)
insight: An activation rate in the 5-15% range often correlates better with long-term retention than a higher percentage.
pull_quote: A low activation rate, perhaps 5-15%, is better than a high one. It often means a stronger correlation with long-term retention. You're working to get users to a state they're not reaching today, impacting future growth.

episode_title: Inside OpenAI | Logan Kilpatrick (head of developer relations)
insight: Prioritize candidates with high agency and urgency; empower teams to solve problems autonomously and ship faster without consensus delays.
pull_quote: High agency and urgency are top characteristics for hires. Empower people to solve problems directly, bypassing 50-person consensus. This allows teams to take on the world and ship incredibly fast.

episode_title: Leveraging growth advisors, mastering SEO, and honing your craft | Luc Levesque (Shopify, Meta)
insight: The right growth advisor can deliver company-changing impact, providing insights that generate hundreds or even thousands of percent lift.
pull_quote: The right growth advisor can have literally company-changing impact. I've seen hundreds of percentages, sometimes over a thousand percent lift when you get it right.

episode_title: Gain attention as an underdog with this framework | Lulu Cheng Meservey
insight: For startups, hyper-target a small, passionate audience to maximize impact and foster deep relationships with limited resources.
pull_quote: To maximize impact with limited resources, decrease your communication 'surface area.' Hyper-target fewer people with a sharper message, fostering deep relationships. Get 10 diehards first; don't try to win over the general public immediately.

episode_title: Pricing your AI product: Lessons from 400+ companies and 50 unicorns | Madhavan Ramanujam
insight: Monetize AI products early and capture value to prevent anchoring customers to low prices and hindering future growth.
pull_quote: If you bring a lot of value with AI but underprice from day one, you train customers to expect more for less. AI products can tap into 10X larger labor budgets; don't anchor yourself on a low price point.

episode_title: The art and science of pricing | Madhavan Ramanujam (Monetizing Innovation, Simon-Kucher)
insight: AI product winners must master monetization from day one to capture value and avoid anchoring low prices, ensuring profitable growth.
pull_quote: The winners in AI will need to master monetization from day one. If you're bringing a lot of value and start training customers to expect $20 a month, you're in trouble.

episode_title: Mastering product strategy and growing as a PM | Maggie Crowley (Toast, Drift, TripAdvisor)
insight: A great PM takes full ownership of outcomes, doing whatever it takes to succeed, even tasks outside their defined job.
pull_quote: If you ever find yourself saying, 'that's not my job,' that's probably a thing you should do. PMs are responsible for outcomes, often the emotional center of the team, and must do whatever it takes to keep products successful.

episode_title: Becoming more strategic, navigating difficult colleagues, founder mode, more | Anneka Gupta
insight: Before seeking product-market fit, ensure 'company-product fit': does it leverage your company's unique strengths and portfolio?
pull_quote: Many companies waste time. Before building, confirm 'company-product fit.' Does this new product truly fit your portfolio and play to your unique strengths? If not, don't do it.

episode_title: 'Behind the founder: Marc Benioff'
insight: Experiment broadly with multiple tactics to identify what resonates, then double down to build your winning strategy.
pull_quote: Throw everything against the wall and see what sticks. Identify the winning tactic. Then, turn that into your winning strategy and grow it.

episode_title: AI and product management | Marily Nika (Meta, Google)
insight: Always identify a real problem or user pain point before deciding to implement an AI solution.
pull_quote: Don't do AI for the sake of doing AI. Identify a clear problem or pain point needing a smart solution first. An AI Product Manager's true role is solving the right problem, not just building AI products.

episode_title: Product management theater | Marty Cagan (Silicon Valley Product Group)
insight: Shift your team's focus from delivering output (shipping features) to solving customer and business problems by measuring actual outcomes.
pull_quote: Strong product companies understand it's all about outcomes. You don't get points for shipping; you get points for delivering value. Frame it as time to money, not time to market.

episode_title: The disease of process people | Marty Cagan
insight: Many individuals with product manager titles are merely performing a project management role, dramatically overpaid for their actual value.
