pull_quote: Many product managers are dramatically overpaid for the value they provide, because their role is essentially project management. It is far easier to deliver output than it is to deliver real outcomes.

episode_title: How to speak more confidently and persuasively | Matt Abrahams (professor, speaker, author)
insight: To excel in stakeholder management, prepare to be spontaneous, honing your ability to communicate effectively on the spot.
pull_quote: You actually have to prepare to be spontaneous. Mastering impromptu communication is crucial for effective stakeholder alignment, giving feedback, and persuasion.

episode_title: The surprising truth about what closes deals: Insights from 2.5m sales conversations | Matt Dixon
insight: Shift from diagnosing customer needs to confidently recommending what they should do, reducing their fear of making the wrong choice.
pull_quote: When buyers are stuck, don't just ask what they want. Offer a clear recommendation. This shifts the burden of a bad decision, making them feel safer and more confident in taking action.

episode_title: The one question that saves product careers | Matt LeMay
insight: Set team goals no more than one step away from company goals to ensure direct business impact.
pull_quote: Set team goals no more than one step away from company goals. Avoid cascading goals into oblivion; ensure direct alignment. This empowers teams to make bolder decisions and secure needed resources by demonstrating clear business impact.

episode_title: "I deliberately understaff every project" | Leadership lessons from Rippling's $16B journey
insight: Deliberately understaff projects to prevent politics, prioritize core work, and avoid unnecessary waste and slowdowns.
pull_quote: Deliberately understaff every project. Overstaffing leads to politics, working on low-priority items, waste, and slows you down. Under-staffing is less evil and ensures focus.

episode_title: Are your fears giving you terrible advice? | Matt Mochary
insight: Fewer people in an organization often leads to better performance and faster output by reducing coordination overhead and friction.
pull_quote: After layoffs, CEOs reported: 'It's insane. The company's now operating better. We're putting out more features, more code, NPS is up.' Fewer people reduce coordination issues, leading to absolute performance improvement.

episode_title: The one question that saves product careers | Matt LeMay
insight: Prioritize going back to basics, improving user experience, and ruthlessly editing or cutting less relevant features.
pull_quote: We have some variable quality around some of our things. This year, we're focusing on going back to basics, improving user experience, and ruthlessly editing and cutting as much as possible.

episode_title: How to tell better stories | Matthew Dicks (Storyworthy)
insight: To be memorable in business, speak in stories; our minds forget facts and data unless they are anchored to vivid imagery.
pull_quote: Most business communication is round, white, and flavorless, making you forgettable. Our minds don't remember facts or data without imagery. Tell stories, or 100% your message will be forgotten.

episode_title: Building Anchor, selling to Spotify, and lessons learned | Maya Prohovnik (Head of Podcast Product)
insight: To gain rapid market share, eliminate user friction with 'magical' experiences, even if they rely on unscalable manual processes initially.
pull_quote: We had college students making Apple podcast accounts and submitting hundreds of thousands of podcasts through these accounts. People thought we had a secret deal, but it was just manual, unscalable work that felt magical to users.

episode_title: Unconventional product lessons from Binance, N26, Google, more | Mayur Kamat (CPO at N26)
insight: Redefine product strategy as rapid hypothesis testing and data validation to accelerate learning and execution.
pull_quote: Strategy is a little bit overrated for product. Your strategy should be, 'How fast can I go from hypothesis to data?' The faster you go from hypothesis to data, the easier your strategy gets.

episode_title: Lessons from Atlassian | Megan Cook (Head of Product, Jira)
insight: To launch new products, use a staged 'Wonder, Explore, Make, Impact, Scale' process to iterate fast and scale investment.
pull_quote: We created small groups with stage gates: Wonder, Explore, Make, Impact, Scale. This assesses bets at every stage, iterating quickly. It helps decide fast if we should invest more or stop, crucial for successful new product development.

episode_title: She turned 100+ rejections into a $42B company | Melanie Perkins
insight: Systematically collect and act on customer requests, augmented by frequent user testing, to deeply understand and meet user needs.
pull_quote: Canva gets over a million community requests annually. Systematically tally, deliver, and close those loops. Supplement explicit asks with frequent user testing; even 10 random people can give astute, representative feedback.

episode_title: Building high-performing teams | Melissa Tan (Webflow, Dropbox, Canva)
insight: Most organizations mistakenly focus on team training; 99% of product issues stem from poor goal setting and strategy deployment.
pull_quote: 99% of the time, product issues are not about training people, but about how goals are set and strategy is deployed. Without clear context, even trained teams lack direction.

episode_title: Everything you’ve ever wanted to know about SAFe and the product owner role | Melissa Perri
insight: Prioritize customer value and business outcomes over rigid process adherence to avoid building the wrong things.
pull_quote: Many organizations get so caught up in agile processes that they forget to ask, 'Are we serving customers or winning in the market?' Focus on achieving real business value, not just perfect sprints or framework compliance.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to product operations | Melissa Perri and Denise Tilles
insight: Establish a product operations team to streamline operational work, allowing product managers to dedicate more time to strategic value creation and scaling.
pull_quote: Do you want to hire 10,000 product managers doing operational work 70% of the time, or build a product ops team creating shared systems so PMs can focus on strategy?

episode_title: Building high-performing teams | Melissa Tan (Webflow, Dropbox, Canva)
insight: Hiring first-principles thinkers, even without direct experience, drives innovation by encouraging fresh, unconstrained problem-solving.
pull_quote: Dropbox hired smart people with no sales experience and tasked them with sales. This led to tons of innovation because first-principles thinkers, unburdened by past methods, just figured things out.

episode_title: An inside look at Deel’s unprecedented growth | Meltem Kuran Berkowitz (Head of Growth)
insight: For early B2B product success, prioritize a strong product and engineering team, as acquisition channels fail without a quality core.
pull_quote: Acquisition channels fail if your product doesn't meet expectations. Early B2B companies need a strong product and engineering core. If you see six salespeople and two engineers, it signals a weak foundation.

episode_title: How to work through fear, give hard feedback, and doing layoffs with grace | Matt Mochary
insight: True product-led growth requires immediate, day-zero value, otherwise the product's ease of entry becomes its ease of exit.
pull_quote: Not every product can be product-led. If your tool doesn't offer day-zero value, the easier you make it to come in, the easier it can also be to leave. Ensure immediate utility for sustained adoption.

episode_title: The rise of Cursor: The $300M ARR AI tool that engineers can’t stop using | Michael Truell
insight: Unexpectedly, developing custom models became critical for Cursor's 'magic moments,' directly enhancing product quality and competitive advantage.
pull_quote: We didn't expect to build our own models. But every magic moment in Cursor involves a custom model. This was counterintuitive yet critical for product quality and competitive edge.

episode_title: 'Vision, conviction, and hype: How to build 0 to 1 inside a company | Mihika Kapoor (Product, Figma)'
insight: For new products, proactively build and sustain internal and external excitement to overcome challenges and ensure the project successfully launches.
pull_quote: When spearheading a new product, the onus is on you to constantly build internal hype. Rally your company by demonstrating potential early, ensuring excitement to get it out the door.

episode_title: Anthropic's CPO on what comes next | Mike Krieger (co-founder of Instagram)
insight: AI founders should build defensible products with differentiated industry knowledge, go-to-market strategies, or novel interface form factors.
pull_quote: Defensible areas for AI startups include deep understanding of a particular market, differentiated go-to-market with customer relationships, or completely new takes on the form factor for interfacing with AI.

episode_title: Pattern Breakers: How to find a breakthrough startup idea | Mike Maples, Jr. (Partner at Floodgate)
insight: To find breakthrough ideas, live in the future, actively observe what's missing, and trust your unique intuition.
pull_quote: If you're not living in the future, your opinion about it isn't valid. To find breakthrough ideas, don't think of a startup; live in the future and notice what's missing.

episode_title: “I like being scared”: Molly Graham’s frameworks for rapid career growth | Molly Graham
insight: True prioritization in goal-setting demands painful trade-offs and assigning a single owner to each goal for clear accountability.
pull_quote: Strategy should hurt. If your goal setting process is not painful, you are not prioritizing heavily enough. One goal must have one owner; if you cannot do that, you haven't done the most important work.

episode_title: How Palantir built the ultimate founder factory | Nabeel S. Qureshi (founder, writer, ex-Palantir)
insight: Prioritize solving a customer's massive burning problem, even if it means pivoting from your initial product vision.
pull_quote: Founders often err by sticking too rigidly to their initial product vision. It's often the right call to pivot and put everything on that big problem instead.

episode_title: Linear’s secret to building beloved B2B products | Nan Yu (Head of Product)
insight: Uncover non-obvious solutions by probing customer requests to identify the specific 'bad feeling' they want to eliminate, then build for that emotion.
pull_quote: My goal is to feel bad in the same way that customers feel bad. What is the actual emotional valence motivating your request? Then build to ensure they never have that bad feeling again.

episode_title: Storytelling with Nancy Duarte: How to craft compelling presentations and tell a story that sticks
insight: Utilize the 'what is, what could be, new bliss' framework to create desire for your proposed future in any moment of influence.
pull_quote: The 'what is, what could be, new bliss' framework is a powerful tool. Use this contrast as a mental model in any moment of influence, from big presentations to quick phone calls, to make people long for your future vision.

episode_title: Meta’s head of product on working with Mark Zuckerberg, early growth tactics, and more | Naomi Gleit
insight: Establish a single, canonical document for every project to ensure extreme clarity on workstreams, owners, processes, and key information.
pull_quote: Ask five different people, get five different answers? Unacceptable. Create one canonical doc for every project. Everyone knows exactly where to find all information, driving extreme clarity.

episode_title: How to price your product | Naomi Ionita (Menlo Ventures)
insight: Treat your product's pricing like your roadmap; regularly revisit and iterate on your monetization strategy every 6-12 months.
pull_quote: Don't set and forget your pricing. Many companies labor over features but neglect pricing after launch. Revisit your monetization strategy every 6-12 months, just like your product roadmap, to ensure you're compensated appropriately.

episode_title: 'Inside ChatGPT: The fastest growing product in history | Nick Turley (OpenAI)'
insight: For emergent AI products, ship quickly to learn what's possible and what users want; prior reasoning is often insufficient.
pull_quote: With AI, you won't know what to polish until after you ship. You really have to ship to understand what's possible and what people want, rather than being able to reason about it a priori.

episode_title: Nickey Skarstad (Airbnb, Etsy, Shopify, Duolingo) on translating vision into goals, operationalizing product quality, second-order decisions, brainstorming, influence, and much more
insight: Adding friction to onboarding, like requiring thoughtful listings, can improve long-term user success and retention by ensuring early wins.
pull_quote: We added friction to onboarding to slow sellers down, making them more thoughtful about listings. This helped them achieve a first sale faster, a critical quality metric. Focus on early user success, not just rapid sign-ups.

episode_title: How to measure AI developer productivity in 2025 | Nicole Forsgren
insight: Align your AI productivity metrics with specific leadership priorities like market share or profit margin to demonstrate clear business value.
pull_quote: Don't blindly apply productivity metrics; tailor them to leadership's priorities. If they care about market share, measure speed to production. If profit margin, quantify vendor or cloud cost savings.

episode_title: How to measure AI developer productivity in 2025 | Nicole Forsgren
insight: Fast development with AI demands stronger strategy to ensure teams build the right things, avoiding faster delivery of wrong solutions.
pull_quote: We can ship trash faster every single day. We need strategy and really smart decisions to know what to ship.

episode_title: Building a long and meaningful career | Nikhyl Singhal (Meta, Google)
insight: Senior product leaders must identify how their strengths cast 'shadows,' hindering growth by creating blind spots in their interactions.
pull_quote: Your 'superpowers' may be creating 'shadows' that hinder your growth. Revisit contradictory feedback you've dismissed. It often reveals how your greatest strengths are now holding you back.

episode_title: Driving alignment within teams, work-life balance, and the changing PM landscape | Nikita Miller
insight: When testing a new product, manually ensure the best possible user experience to eliminate confounding variables and get clear signal.
pull_quote: When testing, eliminate confounding variables and test the best possible version, even if it's manual and won't scale. This clarifies if people care about the core value, ensuring your experiments provide undeniable signal.

episode_title: Driving alignment within teams, work-life balance, and the changing PM landscape | Nikita Miller
insight: Don't swing too far on outcomes; remember that shipping things to market quickly is a crucial indicator of successful product development.
pull_quote: Many teams over-focus on outcomes, forgetting output. If you're not shipping a lot quickly, all your ideation and decision-making efforts don't matter much. Execution and velocity are critical.

episode_title: How to drive word of mouth | Nilan Peiris (CPO of Wise)
insight: To drive word-of-mouth, deliver an experience so unexpectedly superior that it truly blows your users' socks off.
pull_quote: Users only become advocates when a product is 8 to 10 times better, not incrementally. To get genuine recommendation, your product must blow users' socks off by offering an experience they didn't know was possible.

episode_title: Strategies for becoming less distractible and improving focus | Nir Eyal
insight: A more valuable metric for productivity is 'Did I do what I said I was going to do for as long as I said I would without distraction,' rather than merely task completion.
pull_quote: You can't call something a distraction unless you know what it distracted you from. Measure your personal productivity by whether you did what you planned, without distraction, not just if a task was finished. This ensures time for deep, reflective work and accurate planning.

episode_title: The 10 traits of great PMs, AI, and Slack’s approach to product | Noah Weiss (Slack, Google)
insight: If growth plateaus, sacrifice short-term impact for long-term learning to discover new customer needs and unblock growth.
pull_quote: Slack's self-service business plateaued in 2019. To restart growth, they "threw the whole roadmap away," optimizing for learning over impact for six months to find new levers. This led to doubling paid customer growth by understanding new users.

episode_title: The happiness and pain of product management | Noam Lovinsky (Grammarly, FB, Thumbtack, YT)
insight: Avoid relying on a single growth channel; diversify early to protect your business from sudden traffic or acquisition changes.
pull_quote: “One channel growth is always a no-no.” Thumbtack saw triple-digit growth fall to negative year-over-year after SEO changes. Diversify your acquisition channels before they dry up.

episode_title: Picking sharp problems, increasing virality, and unique product frameworks | Oji Udezue (Typeform)
insight: Embed customer conversations into team calendars weekly so PMs, designers, EMs, and PMMs continuously engage by default.
pull_quote: You should organize it so that your PMs, your designers, your engineering managers and your PMMs are constantly talking to customers by default. Customer conversations show up on their calendar every week automagically.

episode_title: How to ask the right questions, project confidence, and win over skeptics | Paige Costello (Asana)
insight: Combat scarcity mindset by asking "How might the opposite be true?" to uncover alternative paths and reduce mental load.
pull_quote: Challenge your scarcity mindset by asking, 'How might the opposite be true?' This question helps create alternative options and shows a different path, reducing stress and enabling you to tackle more.

episode_title: 10 lessons on bootstrapping a $200m business | Patrick Campbell (ProfitWell)
insight: Aligning on a clear 'tempo framework' for shipping frequency is more crucial for team output than optimizing organizational design.
pull_quote: Your tempo framework is more important than your org design. Real professionals ship at high frequency. Align your team on what 'good' looks like in terms of shipping tempo to create a truly high-output culture.

episode_title: Redefining success, money, and belonging | Paul Millerd (The Pathless Path)
insight: To build a new path, meticulously track what energizes or saps your energy; then consciously do more of what energizes you.
pull_quote: Pay really close attention to what gives you energy after you do the thing. Do more of the things that energize you and do less of the things that de-energize you. This simple strategy can help you discover a new, fulfilling path.

episode_title: Founder-led sales | Pete Kazanjy (Founding Sales, Atrium)
insight: Treat your sales motion like software; continuously update its "source code" with new slides, scripts, and objections based on customer feedback.
pull_quote: A sales motion is like software; constantly update it. That interesting prospect question with no good answer? Make a slide. Now your sales motion is updated and more effective.

episode_title: Peter Deng
insight: Your product's pixels don't always matter; the true product often encompasses holistic elements like price or delivery.
pull_quote: Sometimes your product actually doesn't matter. At Uber, the price and ETA were the real product. We humans consume the entirety of the product, not just the digital manifestation.

episode_title: How to be the best coach to product people | Petra Wille (Strong Product People)
insight: Strong storytelling and public speaking are essential for PM career advancement, rallying teams, and avoiding career stagnation.
pull_quote: Not being good at public speaking and bringing your point across is a career staller for product managers. It's crucial for rallying teams behind shared goals and securing promotions.

episode_title: Land your dream job in today’s market: negotiation tactics, job search councils, more | Phyl Terry
insight: Proactively define your role's mission and OKRs to deeply understand and influence your contribution, impressing stakeholders and clarifying success.
pull_quote: No one in my life has ever done this. If someone did this, it would blow my mind. I would hire them on the spot.

episode_title: Building Wiz: the fastest-growing startup in history | Raaz Herzberg (CMO and VP Product Strategy)
insight: Real product-market fit is signaled by customers asking about pricing, next steps, and internal connections, not just polite interest.
pull_quote: Polite interest isn't product-market fit. We knew we had it when customer calls changed from 'sounds interesting' to 'how are you pricing this? When can we start a POV? Who should I connect you to?' They pushed for the next step.

episode_title: A guide to difficult conversations, building high-trust teams, and designing a life you love
insight: Shift from advising to coaching your team to empower them, accelerate execution, and free yourself for critical strategic work.
pull_quote: An AI company CEO shifted from making every product decision to coaching his tech leads. Squads moved faster, teams felt empowered, and he gained time for 2026 strategic planning.

episode_title: Superhuman's secret to success | Rahul Vohra (CEO and founder)
insight: To systematically increase product-market fit, prioritize feedback from 'somewhat disappointed' users who value your core offering.
pull_quote: Most companies struggle because they don't know who to listen to. To increase product-market fit, ignore the "not disappointed" and focus on "somewhat disappointed" users whose main benefit resonates. This segment provides the most actionable feedback.

episode_title: Marketplace lessons from Uber, Airbnb, Bumble, and more | Ramesh Johari (Stanford professor)
insight: Consequential marketplace changes create winners and losers; prioritize changes where the winners are more vital to your business than the losers.
pull_quote: Marketplaces are a game of whac-a-mole: many consequential changes create winners and losers. Rolling with these changes means recognizing if the winners you've created are more important than the losers.

episode_title: How to build your product strategy stack | Ravi Mehta (Tinder, Facebook, Tripadvisor, Outpace)
insight: Set goals after defining your product roadmap and strategy, ensuring they measure progress toward a clear destination, not arbitrary targets.
pull_quote: Don't start with goals; define your strategy and roadmap first, then set goals as progress markers. Without a destination, "driving 250 miles" means nothing; goals need strategic context.

episode_title: Product management theater | Marty Cagan (Silicon Valley Product Group)
insight: Deep collaboration between engineering, product, sales, and data science is crucial to build the right product and achieve market success.
pull_quote: Engineers, product, sales, and data science must collaborate closely. Take PMs and engineers on client immersion trips to feel the market's pain points. This direct feedback loop is TikTok's secret sauce for building successful products.

episode_title: Good Strategy, Bad Strategy | Richard Rumelt
insight: Refocus strategy from grand vision to a concrete action agenda addressing one or two addressable, key challenges.
pull_quote: Don't call it strategy, call it an action agenda. Identify the one or two key challenges that can actually be addressed, and then focus on the coherent action steps you'll take to deal with them.

episode_title: Inside Google's AI turnaround: AI Mode, AI Overviews, and vision for AI-powered search | Robby Stein
insight: Product success comes from relentless, productive effort, fueled by deep dissatisfaction with the status quo, to make things better daily.
pull_quote: My wife described me as 'dissatisfied.' It's not unhappiness, but a deep desire to make things better. This relentless drive, being your own harshest critic, creates a compounding effect until the product truly tips into usefulness.

episode_title: 5 essential questions to craft a winning strategy | Roger Martin (author, advisor, speaker)
insight: Winning products need defensible capabilities that are hard for competitors to replicate, not just 'better' features.
pull_quote: Just being better isn't enough; your product's competitive advantage must be hard for others to replicate quickly. Build capabilities competitors can't or won't match.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to A/B testing | Ronny Kohavi (Airbnb, Microsoft, Amazon)
insight: Most experiments show no gain; do not ship features with flat results unless legally required, to avoid unnecessary code complexity.
pull_quote: 92% of our ideas failed to improve key metrics. If an experiment shows no statistically significant improvement, don't ship it. You've just complicated the code base with no value, increasing maintenance costs.

episode_title: A better way to plan, build, and ship products | Ryan Singer (creator of “Shape Up")
insight: Prioritize generating revenue earlier, even alongside growth, to gain financial flexibility and prove your business model.
pull_quote: I would've actually tried to monetize and generated revenue sooner. We could've been cashflow break-even much earlier, owning our own destiny.

episode_title: The role of AI in new product development | Ryan J. Salva (VP of Product at GitHub)
insight: To transition R&D moonshots to core products, embed researchers with product teams then let new teams own the roadmap and operationalize.
pull_quote: To successfully scale R&D projects, don't just hand them off. Embed researchers with the new product team for knowledge transfer, then ensure the product team fully owns the roadmap. Only transition researchers back after replacement.

episode_title: A better way to plan, build, and ship products | Ryan Singer (creator of “Shape Up")
insight: Proactively involve experienced engineers in product shaping to expose hidden technical complexities and enable crucial trade-offs early.
pull_quote: Many projects blow out because hidden technical complexities are discovered too late. Bring a 'grumpy old plumber' engineer into shaping sessions. They insist on opening up the walls to inspect the pipes, preventing costly surprises.

episode_title: Building Substack | Sachin Monga (Substack, Facebook)
insight: Maximize user control in network effects by enabling direct, curated recommendations, leveraging goodwill to drive viral growth.
pull_quote: We picked that way [writer-controlled recommendations] even if it might seem harder to pull off. There was this virality at play now where when you recommend a bunch of people, those people will get an email... It created this goodwill viral loop.

episode_title: How to hit revenue targets in a recession | Sahil Mansuri (Bravado)
insight: In a challenging market, move your best salespeople to customer success to relentlessly focus on retaining every existing customer.
pull_quote: New sales are nearly impossible right now. Reallocate your top sales talent to Customer Success. Your priority: ensure every existing customer stays, because replacing them will be impossible.

episode_title: How to be more innovative | Sam Schillace (Microsoft deputy CTO, creator of Google Docs)
insight: Users are inherently lazy; products succeed by providing significant, undeniable value that makes life genuinely easier.
pull_quote: Users are lazy. Convenience always wins. No one will do something for any other reason than it makes their life better. It has to make your life a lot better, hopefully 10X better than what you spend to use it.

episode_title: Why Uber’s CPO delivers food on weekends | Sachin Kansal
insight: For bold new products, prioritize intentionality and launch decisively, then iterate from the new baseline instead of endless upfront A/B testing.
pull_quote: For new products, founders often go off gut, not data. They cut first, then iterate from a new baseline. Don't measure and cut; cut and then iterate from the new baseline.

episode_title: AI prompt engineering in 2025: What works and what doesn’t | Sander Schulhoff
insight: Prompt injection is an unsolvable problem, necessitating continuous AI security vigilance in product development.
pull_quote: Prompt injection is not a solvable problem for AI products. If we can't even trust chatbots to be secure, how can we trust agents to manage our finances or walk around in humanoid robots?

episode_title: Why securing AI is harder than anyone expected and guardrails are failing | HackAPrompt CEO
insight: External AI guardrails are ineffective; build AI products securely by integrating classical cybersecurity for data access and action permissioning.
pull_quote: AI guardrails do not work. Relying on them won't protect your product. Instead, integrate classical cybersecurity and AI understanding to properly permission data and actions within your system.

episode_title: The hierarchy of engagement | Sarah Tavel (Benchmark, Greylock, Pinterest)
insight: Marketplaces must laser-focus on a small, constrained market to build happy, retentive users, enabling future expansion and dominance.
pull_quote: GMV is a vanity metric; prioritize 'happy GMV'. Focus your ambition like a laser beam on a small 'thimble' market to get product-market fit. Heat that up really hot, then it expands from there.

episode_title: Lessons on product sense, AI, the first mile experience, and the messy middle | Scott Belsky (Adobe)
insight: Optimize for problems you want to have later by ruthlessly cutting features and focusing on core value in your initial product.
pull_quote: Scott Belsky's Behance scaled by removing features. Optimize for the problems you want to have, not for features customers will only ask for later. Making the product about one thing dramatically increases your core metric velocity.

episode_title: 'Inside Devin: The AI engineer that''s set to write 50% of its company’s code this year | Scott Wu'
insight: AI engineers are set to write over 50% of our company's code this year, fundamentally shifting engineering capacity.
pull_quote: A quarter of our pull requests today are committed by Devins. We expect this to be over 50% by the end of the year, with a team of only 15 engineers.

episode_title: The original growth hacker reveals his secrets | Sean Ellis (author of “Hacking Growth”)
insight: Optimize internal product efficiency – especially activation – before scaling acquisition; it makes growth 100x more effective.
pull_quote: LogMeIn froze product development to focus solely on activation. They boosted user engagement by 1000%. This enabled acquisition channels to scale 100x, from $10k to $1M/month, making growth sustainably profitable.

episode_title: Seth Godin's best tactics for building remarkable products, strategies, brands and more
insight: Be truly remarkable, creating products people inherently want to talk about, because word of mouth is the key to winning.
pull_quote: The word remarkable means worth making a remark about. If you make something where the person's life gets better if they talk about you and you know in advance what you want them to say, then they are more likely to say it.

episode_title: Why great AI products are all about the data | Shaun Clowes (CPO at Confluent)
insight: Consistently prioritize external customer, market, and competitor perspectives to identify unique, differentiated value, avoiding internal focus.
pull_quote: Spend 80% of your time thinking outside the building. Always talk from the customer's, market's, and competitor's perspectives to find reliable, differentiated value. PMs get better almost immediately.

episode_title: The rituals of great teams | Shishir Mehrotra, Coda, YouTube, Microsoft
insight: Find the single, higher-level 'eigenquestion' that, once answered, makes many subsequent product decisions easy.
pull_quote: An 'eigenquestion' is a higher-level question that, if answered, makes many other decisions easy. For YouTube, asking if the market values 'consistency or comprehensiveness' resolved linking dilemmas and aligned the team.

episode_title: The art of product management | Shreyas Doshi (Stripe, Twitter, Google, Yahoo)
insight: Most perceived execution issues in high-performing teams are actually symptoms of underlying strategy, interpersonal, or cultural problems.
pull_quote: Most execution problems are actually not execution problems. They're usually deeper strategy, interpersonal, or cultural issues. This shifts how you solve problems, focusing on root causes.

episode_title: The art of product management | Shreyas Doshi (Stripe, Twitter, Google, Yahoo)
insight: Most seemingly two-way product decisions are actually one-way doors; invest more time in critical thinking before committing.
pull_quote: Most doors that look like two-way doors are actually one-way doors. These quick decisions accumulate feature debt. Thinking is cheap; invest more time in deeper evaluation before committing.

episode_title: Product lessons from Waymo | Shweta Shrivastava (Waymo, Amazon, Cisco)
insight: You must disrupt your own product and business model before someone else does, as disruption is inevitable.
pull_quote: The classic innovator's dilemma: large companies can get very incremental in product strategy. You need to disrupt yourself before somebody else does, because it's going to happen. It's inevitable.

episode_title: Lessons from scaling Ramp | Sri Batchu (Ramp, Instacart, Opendoor)
insight: Design experiments to fail conclusively by maximizing treatment effect, ensuring clear learning to avoid repeating unfruitful tests.
pull_quote: Failure isn't not driving revenue; it's not learning. Maximize your experiment's treatment effect, throwing all tactics at it. If it fails, you learn definitively to move on; if it works, you optimize later.

episode_title: Hot takes and techno-optimism from tech’s top power couple | Sriram and Aarthi
insight: Aggressively optimize for core retention metrics by driving network effects, even if it slightly degrades individual user experience.
pull_quote: Facebook aggressively optimized for 10 friends in 14 days. Sometimes, this meant making one user's experience slightly worse to help another reach that crucial retention milestone. Focus on driving network effects, not just individual jobs-to-be-done.

episode_title: Mental models for building products people love ft. Stewart Butterfield
insight: Prioritize reducing user cognitive load over merely reducing clicks; eliminate friction by making the product trivially easy to comprehend.
pull_quote: Don't just reduce friction or clicks; focus on making software simple and preventing users from thinking. Making users feel stupid due to incomprehensible choices is worse than extra, trivially easy steps.

episode_title: Lessons in product leadership and AI strategy from Glean, Google, Amazon, and Slack | Tamar Yehoshua
insight: To avoid falling behind in AI, actively use various AI products and creatively apply them to your daily PM tasks.
pull_quote: Don't get left behind in AI. Actively use products like ChatGPT, Glean, or Claude. Explore creative applications, like analyzing Discord transcripts for sentiment or summarizing articles. Leverage AI for productivity.

episode_title: Hard-won lessons building 0 to 1 inside Atlassian | Tanguy Crusson (Head of Jira Product Discovery)
insight: Validate demand for a new product with real user interest before writing any code to avoid building the wrong thing.
pull_quote: For Jira Product Discovery, we validated demand before writing code. A waitlist yielded 3000+ signups in two weeks, confirming interest. This simple step avoids building features nobody wants.

episode_title: Teaser_2021
insight: True product building involves persistent effort through the 'messy middle,' embracing setbacks and actively seeking feedback.
pull_quote: The biggest gap in business is the 'river of shit' between starting and finishing. It's getting punched in the gut daily, but being willing to push through, get feedback, and work with your team. No one talks about this messy middle.

episode_title: Build better products with continuous product discovery | Teresa Torres
insight: Improve user research by collecting customer stories about past actions and experiences, not hypothetical thoughts or future behavior.
pull_quote: Most product teams ask direct, out-of-context questions. This leads to unreliable answers. Instead, ask customers to tell specific stories about what they actually did, eliciting needs they aren't even aware of.

episode_title: Inside Etsy’s product, growth, and marketplace evolution | Tim Holley (VP of Product)
insight: Communicating shared values, like carbon offsetting, through simple text can surprisingly boost conversion and resonate deeply with buyers.
pull_quote: A simple line of text in the cart, "Etsy offsets carbon emissions from every delivery," unexpectedly drove huge conversion uplift. It really resonated with buyers and their values, having a shocking, outsized impact.

episode_title: The ultimate guide to paid growth | Timothy Davis (Shopify)
insight: Avoid paid growth investments before achieving product-market fit to prevent user annoyance and wasted resources on non-converting products.
pull_quote: Running paid ads pre-product market fit will only annoy users and waste resources. If your product isn't converting due to fit issues, fix that first before scaling paid campaigns; a bad experience can deter future interest.

episode_title: Tobi Lütke's leadership playbook: First principles, infinite games, and maximizing human potential
insight: Focus on unquantifiable things like fun and delight; if they thrive, metrics will eventually follow.
pull_quote: Optimism sounds naive, metrics-driven sophisticated. But 80% of product value is unquantifiable. If all metrics point down but everyone's having fun, expect metrics to rise next. Fun and delight are powerful leading indicators.

episode_title: A framework for finding product-market fit | Todd Jackson (First Round Capital)
insight: At nascent product-market fit, prioritize extreme customer satisfaction over demand and efficiency to uncover core value.
pull_quote: At Level 1 product-market fit, it's okay to be inefficient. Focus on delivering insane customer satisfaction, like Vanta, who manually fulfilled needs to prove core value.

episode_title: Billion dollar failures, and billion dollar success | Tom Conrad (Quibi, Pandora, Pets.com, Zero)
insight: No amount of product iteration or execution can save a product if its fundamental business equation is broken.
pull_quote: Companies are like a math problem. If the equation is fundamentally broken, no amount of product iteration and execution can get you out of the failed outputs of the broken equation.

episode_title: Why AI is disrupting traditional product management | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
insight: Product leaders building AI-first products must control the algorithm's objectives, features, data, and infrastructure, not just the user experience.
pull_quote: You don't control the experience anymore, you control the ingredients. Product leaders must hold the AI pedals—the algorithm's objectives, features, data, and infrastructure—as the guide of the product boat.

episode_title: Why AI is disrupting traditional product management | Tomer Cohen (LinkedIn CPO)
insight: Successful AI adoption requires robust change management, not just tool deployment, emphasizing incentives, motivation, and visible success.
pull_quote: Change management is critical. It's not enough to just give people AI tools; you must build incentives, motivation, and share success examples. Expecting adoption without this doesn't work.

episode_title: Why most public speaking advice is wrong—and how to finally overcome anxiety | Tristan de Montebello
insight: Focus on root causes, not just symptoms, to solve problems effectively and upgrade your product's underlying 'software'.
pull_quote: Don't just fix symptoms. Address root causes to upgrade your 'software.' This changes the game for solving problems and improving everything.

episode_title: An inside look at how CNN builds product | Upasna Gautam
insight: Effective product communication prioritizes deep listening and pausing before reacting, fostering trust and clarity across diverse stakeholders.
pull_quote: The ability to pause before reacting is key to mindful communication and being a successful product manager. Prioritize listening and converse in the language of the listener to build trust.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to crisis management | Uri Levine (Waze co-founder, serial entrepreneur)
insight: For new hires, set a 30-day calendar reminder: if, knowing what you know now, you wouldn't rehire them, fire them immediately.
pull_quote: Every time that you hire someone new, mark your calendars for 30 days down the road and ask yourself one question, knowing what I know today, would I hire this person? If the answer is no, fire them immediately.

episode_title: A founder’s guide to crisis management | Uri Levine (Waze co-founder, serial entrepreneur)
insight: Retention is the single metric for product-market fit; if users return, you create value.
pull_quote: Product-market fit has only one metric: retention. If users are not coming back, you are not creating value.

episode_title: Building a magical AI code editor used by over 1m developers in 4 months: Inside Windsurf
insight: Even with the same AI models, a custom user interface can triple product acceptance by removing interface limitations.
pull_quote: Our autocomplete product had the same ML models. But by porting to a custom IDE with a better UI, our acceptance rate tripled. This proved that the user interface, not just the technology, was the key to unlocking user value.

episode_title: An inside look at how Miro builds product | Varun Parmar (CPO of Miro)
insight: Host regular informal cross-functional sharing events to foster serendipitous collaboration and dramatically accelerate work.
pull_quote: Miro Connect, a bi-monthly informal sharing event, led to an engineer solving a three-month problem in three hours. Create spaces for serendipitous collaboration to unlock unexpected expertise and accelerate work.

episode_title: An inside look at Mixpanel’s product journey | Vijay Iyengar
insight: When expanding, fund new ventures with profits instead of reallocating core product people, to avoid disrupting your main offering.
pull_quote: Don't take people from your core product to start new ventures. Invest profits, not people. Otherwise, you risk disrupting your core and building 'sixth best' products that don't truly accelerate growth.

episode_title: A framework for PM skill development | Vikrama Dhiman (Gojek)
insight: As a PM, effectively guide decisions and address challenges by raising difficult issues without being difficult, and enabling decisions without making them all yourself.
pull_quote: PMs often get carried away by the 'mini CEO' folklore. To succeed, raise difficult issues without being difficult to work with. Get decisions made without making all the decisions yourself.

episode_title: Persuasive communication and managing up | Wes Kao (Maven, altMBA, Section4)
insight: A small upfront investment in clear communication prevents significant downstream friction, saving everyone time and increasing overall efficiency.
pull_quote: A poorly written memo's 'blast radius' is huge. Spending 30 extra seconds on clarity prevents 15 people from wasting time deciphering your message.

episode_title: Persuasive communication and managing up | Wes Kao (Maven, altMBA, Section4)
insight: Anticipate your Most Obvious Objections (MOO) by spending a few minutes considering potential pushback to strengthen your pitch.
pull_quote: MOO stands for Most Obvious Objection. If you spend even two minutes thinking about likely objections, you'll immediately identify them. Don't be blindsided; prepare your counterarguments and strengthen your pitch.

episode_title: The engineering mindset | Will Larson (Carta, Stripe, Uber, Calm, Digg)
insight: Align EM and PM incentives by giving them the same performance rating, fostering shared accountability for overall execution.
pull_quote: My biggest thing has been trying to force this idea that EM/PM pairs are peers and they generally have the same performance rating. This drives a painful but right perspective where incentives are truly shared. A powerful idea for accountability.

episode_title: An inside look at how Figma builds product | Yuhki Yamashita (CPO of Figma)
insight: Master storytelling and synthesis to make product decisions stick and compel action across a constantly distracted organization.
pull_quote: I often talk about memification: when insights become so powerful that leaders spontaneously cite them in meetings, you know you've really done your job. It’s how knowledge transfers and compels action.

episode_title: An inside look at how Figma builds product | Yuhki Yamashita (CPO of Figma)
insight: Learn to synthesize complex information into memorable stories that compel action and foster shared understanding among stakeholders.
pull_quote: A story is only as good as the action it's capable of driving. Make insights 'memmified' so leaders can cite them, driving knowledge transfer and action effectively.

episode_title: How to grow a subscription business | Yuriy Timen (Grammarly, Canva, Airtable)
insight: Early-stage companies should focus on maximizing their single dominant growth engine before attempting premature diversification.
pull_quote: Most companies rely on one dominant growth engine, not a diversified portfolio. Early-stage startups should resist spreading resources too thin; lean into what's already working to build strategic advantage.

episode_title: Lessons from Airtable’s unconventional growth strategy | Zoelle Egner
insight: Over-invest in early customer success for key champions to catalyze internal word-of-mouth and wider adoption for complex products.
pull_quote: Airtable over-invested in early customer success for champions, helping them build their first bases. Successful champions then became internal evangelists, spreading the product and doing sales pitches for us without our direct effort.
